title: Working memo — what builds belonging
body:
Across the data, belonging seems to grow from small, low-pressure acts rather than from formal membership.

The clearest moment is in [[Interview P01 — transcript]]: the "trowel as a welcome". The same pattern shows up in [[Fieldnotes — Riverside Garden (Visit 1)]], where newcomers hover at the edge until someone hands them a tool. [[Maria (pseudonym)]] is the strongest single case of this.

Linked idea → [[Reciprocity as quiet obligation]].

TODO: contrast community gardens with rented allotments — [[Allotments vs community gardens]] (still to write).

title: Reciprocity as quiet obligation
body:
"We take turns watering — nobody asked us to, it just happens" ([[Interview P01 — transcript]]). The watering rota is unspoken but binding: people say they would "feel bad" not returning the favour.

So reciprocity here is less an explicit rule than a quiet obligation. This connects back to [[Working memo — what builds belonging]], and sits in tension with the shed-lock dispute (ownership / who decides).

title: Method note — go-along interviews
body:
From [[Methods for Urban Ethnography]]: walking with participants surfaces place-meanings that sit-down interviews miss.

Next visit: try a go-along with [[Maria (pseudonym)]] at her plot, and see whether the "trowel" story changes when told in place. Compare against the seated transcript [[Interview P01 — transcript]].
